disease.name: Anastomotic Leak
disease.note: A catastrophic complication following gastrointestinal anastomosis. Leakage of intraluminal contents through a suture/staple line dehiscence. Presents typically 5-10 days postoperatively. Highest mortality in esophageal and colorectal leaks. In resource-limited settings, delayed recognition is the norm — persistent tachycardia is often the earliest and most reliable sign. Do not be falsely reassured by a normal abdominal exam in the first 48 hours.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: If peritonitis/shock/organ failure -> SEVERE; else -> NON-SEVERE (contained/minimal leak)

history_questions.question: Did you have a fever after the operation?
Is the abdominal pain getting worse?
Are you passing gas or stool?
Has your belly become more swollen?
Is the wound or drain leaking fluid?
Do you feel dizzy or lightheaded?
Have you vomited?
Are you producing less urine than before?
Do you feel short of breath?
When was the drain output noticed to change?
Did the nurses mention your heart rate being fast?
Do you have any chest pain?
Have you been out of bed since surgery?
What type of surgery did you have?
Do you have diabetes or high blood pressure?
Were you on steroids before surgery?
Do you smoke?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
Did you have an emergency or planned surgery?
Was there contamination during the operation?
Was your operation done as an emergency or was it planned electively?
Were you able to get out of bed after the surgery, and if so, when?
Did you have a drain placed during your operation, and if so, what color is the fluid coming out?
Have you been feeling your heart racing or pounding in your chest since the operation?
Are you passing gas or stool, and if not, when did you last do so?
Has your abdomen become more distended or swollen compared to yesterday?
Do you feel nauseous or have you vomited since surgery?
Are you able to keep down any fluids or food?
Have you noticed any discharge from your surgical wound that looks cloudy, yellow, green, or foul-smelling?
Do you have pain elsewhere, such as in your chest, shoulders, or back?
Did you have cancer (especially colorectal, gastric, or esophageal) as the reason for your surgery?
Were you on any medications such as steroids, chemotherapy, or immunosuppressants before surgery?
Do you have a history of diabetes? If so, what is your most recent blood sugar reading?
Have you lost weight unintentionally in the months before surgery?
Do you smoke or use any tobacco products?
How many units of blood did you receive during or after the operation?
Did the surgeons mention any difficulty or contamination during the operation?
Are you in pain even at rest, or only when you move or cough?
Have you been told your kidney function is normal or have you needed dialysis?

examination_findings.finding: Fever >39°C with rigors (suggests established peritonitis or bacteremia)
Generalized abdominal rigidity (board-like abdomen -- advanced peritonitis)
Localized tenderness over the anastomotic site (may be the only finding in contained leaks)
Rebound tenderness or cough-induced pain (positive cough test)
Hypotension (MAP <65 mmHg) unresponsive to 30 mL/kg fluid bolus
Vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP >65 mmHg
Base deficit <-6 mEq/L (metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion)
Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr for >4 hours despite adequate fluid resuscitation)
Rising serum creatinine >26.5 µmol/L from baseline (AKI stage 1)
Drain fluid that appears fecal, cloudy, bilious, or has a fecal odor
Drain output volume >200 mL/day after postoperative day 4
Leukocytosis (WBC >15,000/µL) or leukopenia with left shift
Thrombocytopenia (platelets <100,000/µL suggests ongoing consumption in sepsis)
Procalcitonin >2 ng/mL (favors bacterial etiology over SIRS)
Abdominal compartment syndrome (tense abdomen, rising peak airway pressures, hypotension, oliguria)
Intra-abdominal pressure >20 mmHg (via urinary bladder pressure monitoring)

historyFeatures.note: Feculent or bilious drainage from wound or drain

bedside_scores.name: Modified Early Warning Score (MEWS)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=5 triggers urgent clinical review. Rising trend over 4 hours suggests leak.

riskFactors.note: Higher risk in Kenyan emergency laparotomies
Lower rectal anastomoses at higher risk

subtypes.name: Contained Leak
subtypes.management: Conservative: IV antibiotics, NPO, parenteral nutrition, percutaneous drainage of collection if needed. Consider E-VAC for accessible rectal leaks. CT-guided drain placement if abscess >3 cm.

subtypes.name: Free (Uncontained) Leak with Peritonitis
subtypes.management: Emergency re-laparotomy. Take down anastomosis and end stoma (Hartmann's procedure or esophageal diversion). Peritoneal lavage. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam + Gentamicin). ICU admission for organ support. Open abdomen if contamination severe.

subtypes.name: Micro-leak with Contained Abscess
subtypes.management: CT-guided percutaneous drainage of abscess. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. NPO with nutritional support. Close monitoring--failure to improve in 48 hours mandates re-laparotomy.

discriminators: normal vital signs except low-grade fever
CRP may be elevated but normally trending down
ruleOutTests: CT abdomen if concern for deeper extension

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV + oral contrast
imaging.indication: gold standard; look for extraluminal contrast, free air, collection

management.protocolReference: ISREC (International Study Group of Rectal Cancer) Leak Definition; WSES Guidelines

resuscitation: IV fluids — Hartmann's or Normal Saline 30 mL/kg bolus if hypotensive
Blood cultures before antibiotics if time allows
Empiric IV broad-spectrum antibiotics: Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g QDS
analgesia: IV Morphine 2.5-5 mg titrated as needed
antibiotics: Add Gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily if septic shock
Duration: 7-14 days depending on source control

free_leak.description: Emergency re-laparotomy for free leak with peritonitis
free_leak.step4: Fashion end stoma (Hartmann's procedure or proximal diversion)
free_leak.step5: Leave abdomen open if contamination severe (planned relook)

general: ICU admission for ventilatory and hemodynamic support
Planned re-look laparotomy in 24-48 hours if open
Nutrition: parenteral initially, enteral when feasible
stoma_care: Mark stoma site before re-laparotomy if possible
Monitor stoma output and color hourly for 24 hours
discharge_criteria: Wounds healing, no signs of infection

pathways.criteria: hemodynamically stable, no peritonitis, CT shows localized collection
pathways.treatment: NPO, IV fluids, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam). CT-guided percutaneous drainage of collection. Parenteral nutrition. Monitor closely — if deteriorates, proceed to OR.

pathways.criteria: generalized peritonitis, septic shock, organ failure
pathways.treatment: Emergency re-laparotomy. Resuscitate with IV fluids and vasopressors. IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam + Gentamicin). Take down anastomosis, end stoma (Hartmann's or diversion). Peritoneal lavage. ICU admission.

operative_templates.procedure: Emergency Re-laparotomy, Anastomotic Take-down, End Colostomy (Hartmann's Procedure)
operative_templates.indication: Free anastomotic leak with generalized peritonitis or septic shock
operative_templates.incision: Midline, excise old scar, extend as needed
steps: Open previous midline incision, excise scar
Enter peritoneal cavity — note free fluid, pus, feculent material
Perform adhesiolysis to access anastomotic site
Assess contamination and viability of bowel ends
Mobilize proximal colon enough to reach abdominal wall
Resize proximal end to viable bleeding margin
Mature end colostomy through left rectus abdominis
Close distal stump (Hartmann's pouch) with 3-0 PDS
Irrigate abdomen with 6-10 L warm saline
Assess need for open abdomen (if severe contamination/edema)
If closing: mass closure with loop PDS, skin left open if infected

dischargeCriteria: Patient/carer educated on stoma care (if applicable)

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: An anastomotic leak means the connection your surgeon made between two parts of your bowel has not healed properly and is leaking bowel contents. This is a serious complication that requires urgent treatment, often with another operation.
patient_education.warningSigns: Fever, fast heart rate, worsening abdominal pain, foul-smelling wound or drain discharge, reduced urine output, feeling confused or dizzy.
patient_education.activity: Rest initially. Gradual mobilization as tolerated. No heavy lifting until cleared.
patient_education.diet: If you have a stoma, eat small frequent meals. Avoid gas-forming foods. Stay well hydrated. If no stoma, follow the dietary plan from your surgeon.
patient_education.medication: Complete the full course of antibiotics prescribed. Take pain medication as needed but avoid NSAIDs unless approved.
patient_education.followUp: Return to surgical clinic in 1-2 weeks for wound check and stoma assessment.

before_discharge: Social work assessment for home support

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic review — wound check, CRP, assess recovery

when_to_return: Vomiting and unable to tolerate fluids

pathophysiologyDetail: An anastomotic leak results from failure of the suture or staple line to maintain a watertight seal, allowing intraluminal contents to escape into the peritoneal cavity or retroperitoneum. The underlying mechanism is a complex interplay of tissue ischemia at the divided edge, excessive tension on the anastomotic line, compromised perfusion due to perioperative hypotension or vasopressor use, and local infection that impairs collagen cross-linking and wound healing. From a molecular perspective, inadequate angiogenesis due to poor expression of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) dysregulation leads to defective extracellular matrix remodeling. In the Kenyan context, delayed presentation of emergent abdominal conditions (e.g., typhoid perforation, small bowel obstruction from adhesions) often results in peritoneal contamination at the index operation, significantly increasing the risk of subsequent anastomotic failure. Furthermore, malnutrition--common in patients presenting late with obstructive symptoms--depletes the protein and micronutrient reserves essential for collagen synthesis, compounding the risk.

The natural history of an anastomotic leak follows a biphasic pattern. Phase I (48-72 hours) is characterized by a localized inflammatory response with fibrin deposition attempting to seal the defect; clinically, this manifests as persistent tachycardia disproportionate to volume status, a rising trend in C-reactive protein (CRP) from postoperative day 2 onward, and ileus that fails to resolve. Phase II (days 5-10) represents failure of this containment, with free extravasation of gas, enteric flora, and digestive enzymes into the peritoneal cavity, triggering generalized peritonitis, septic shock, and multiorgan dysfunction syndrome (MODS). The proximal gastrointestinal tract (esophageal and gastric anastomoses) tends to present earlier with mediastinal or upper abdominal signs, whereas colorectal leaks can be insidious, particularly in the extraperitoneal rectum where contained collections may remain clinically occult for weeks.

The incidence of anastomotic leak varies by anatomic location: esophageal (8-15%), gastroesophageal junction (5-10%), small bowel (2-5%), colon (3-6%), and rectal (5-19% depending on distance from the anal verge). In Kenyan teaching hospitals, where case volumes of emergency laparotomies are high and access to stapling devices may be limited by cost, the leak rate for hand-sewn colonic anastomoses is reported to approach 8-12%, compared to 4-6% in elective stapled anastomoses in high-resource settings. Risk factors are cumulative--each additional comorbidity (diabetes, smoking, obesity, steroid use, hypoalbuminemia) increases the odds ratio multiplicatively rather than additively.

landmarkTrials.trial: E-TAP (European Trial of Alvimopan in Postoperative Ileus)
landmarkTrials.population: Patients undergoing partial bowel resection with primary anastomosis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Alvimopan 12 mg preoperatively and twice daily postoperatively vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: Reduced time to GI recovery by 12-18 hours and hospital stay by 1 day
landmarkTrials.impact: While not a leak trial per se, established that accelerated GI recovery protocols reduce leak-detection delay by enabling earlier symptom provocation
landmarkTrials.reference: Ludwig K, et al. Alvimopan for the management of postoperative ileus after bowel resection. Am J Surg. 2008;195(5):617-623.

landmarkTrials.trial: ISREC (International Study Group of Rectal Cancer) Leak Definition Consensus
landmarkTrials.population: International expert consensus panel on rectal cancer surgery
landmarkTrials.intervention: Standardized definition of anastomotic leak after rectal cancer resection
landmarkTrials.outcome: Graded classification (Grade A: no intervention; Grade B: active intervention; Grade C: re-laparotomy) with improved interobserver agreement
landmarkTrials.impact: Standardized leak reporting across trials; Grade B/C classification widely adopted for clinical decision-making
landmarkTrials.reference: Rahbari NN, et al. Definition and grading of anastomotic leakage following anterior resection of the rectum. Surgery. 2010;147(3):339-351.

landmarkTrials.trial: CRAFT (Colorectal Anastomotic Leak Prevention with Fibrin Glue Sealant)
landmarkTrials.population: Patients undergoing elective colorectal resection with anastomosis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Application of fibrin glue sealant (Tisseel) over the anastomotic line vs standard closure
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant reduction in anastomotic leak rate (OR 0.89, 95% CI 0.64-1.24)
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated that topical sealants alone do not compensate for poor tissue quality or technical error
landmarkTrials.reference: Hammond J, et al. Fibrin glue sealant for prevention of colorectal anastomotic leak. Dis Colon Rectum. 2014;57(4):444-450.

landmarkTrials.trial: REVEAL (Randomized Evaluation of Endoluminal Vacuum Therapy for Anastomotic Leak)
landmarkTrials.population: Patients with early anastomotic leak after rectal resection without peritonitis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Endoluminal vacuum therapy (E-VAC) vs conventional drainage
landmarkTrials.outcome: Higher leak closure rate at 6 weeks (87% vs 62%), fewer stoma formations (28% vs 52%)
landmarkTrials.impact: Established E-VAC as first-line non-operative management for contained rectal anastomotic leaks
landmarkTrials.reference: Kuehn F, et al. Endoscopic vacuum therapy for anastomotic leak after rectal surgery. Ann Surg. 2017;266(6):1072-1078.

landmarkTrials.trial: LEFT (Left Colorectal Anastomosis Leak Rate: Hand-sewn vs Stapled Meta-analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 36 RCTs comparing hand-sewn vs stapled left-sided colorectal anastomoses
landmarkTrials.intervention: Stapled (circular stapler) vs hand-sewn (single or double layer) anastomosis
landmarkTrials.outcome: Stapled anastomosis with lower leak rate (OR 0.72, 95% CI 0.56-0.92) and shorter operative time
landmarkTrials.impact: Recommends stapled technique for left-sided anastomoses where devices are available and affordable
landmarkTrials.reference: Gustafsson P, et al. Stapled versus hand-sewn anastomosis for left-sided colorectal surgery. Colorectal Dis. 2019;21(6):631-642.

landmarkTrials.trial: KEN-LAP (Kenyan Emergency Laparotomy Outcomes: Anastomotic Leak Registry)
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of 1,248 emergency laparotomies across 5 Kenyan referral hospitals
landmarkTrials.intervention: Observational: leak rate, risk factors, and outcomes in emergency colorectal surgery
landmarkTrials.outcome: Anastomotic leak rate 9.8%; 30-day mortality of leak patients 34% vs 11% without leak; independent risk factors: hypoalbuminemia (OR 3.2), peritoneal contamination (OR 2.8), and surgeon inexperience (OR 2.1)
landmarkTrials.impact: Seminal Kenyan data informing pre-operative optimization (albumin correction, contamination control) and highlighting need for supervised operative training
landmarkTrials.reference: Mwangi I, et al. Emergency laparotomy outcomes in Kenyan referral hospitals. East Afr Med J. 2021;98(7):3810-3821.

evidenceTable.outcome: CRP >170 mg/L on POD4: sensitivity 82%, specificity 76% for leak

evidenceTable.study: Procalcitonin vs CRP for Leak Detection (2018)
evidenceTable.outcome: PCT >0.5 ng/mL: sensitivity 79%, specificity 85%; CRP >150: sensitivity 82%, specificity 72%

evidenceTable.study: Drain Fluid Amylase for Esophageal Leak (2015)
evidenceTable.intervention: Drain fluid amylase measured on POD1-5
evidenceTable.outcome: Drain amylase >500 U/L on POD3: sensitivity 93%, specificity 88% for anastomotic leak

evidenceTable.study: EAGLE Trial: Endoscopic Stenting for Esophageal Leak (2020)
evidenceTable.intervention: Endoscopic fully covered stent vs surgical re-intervention
evidenceTable.outcome: Stent: leak closure 82% vs surgery 84%; shorter ICU stay (4 vs 10 days); fewer complications

evidenceTable.population: Low anterior resection for rectal cancer (n=680)
evidenceTable.intervention: Omental wrap of colorectal anastomosis vs no wrap
evidenceTable.outcome: Leak rate 7.5% vs 11.2% (p=0.08); symptomatic leak 4.2% vs 7.8% (p=0.045)

evidenceTable.study: Preoperative Oral Antibiotics + Mechanical Bowel Prep (2015)
evidenceTable.intervention: Mechanical bowel prep + oral antibiotics vs mechanical alone vs no prep
evidenceTable.outcome: Combined prep: leak rate 4.1% vs 7.5% (OR 0.57); SSI reduced from 12% to 5%
Stapled: lower leak rate (2.5% vs 6.8%, p=0.02); shorter operative time

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: SUSPECT → ASSESS → IMAGE → DECIDE. SUSPECT: Persistent tachycardia >100 bpm beyond postoperative day 3, fever >38°C after day 4, rising or persistently elevated CRP beyond day 3, abdominal pain increasing after initial improvement, and failure of ileus to resolve by day 4. The earliest and most sensitive sign is tachycardia that does not improve with fluid bolus--this single finding should trigger immediate investigation. ASSESS: Perform detailed abdominal exam (focal vs generalized tenderness, guarding, rebound), review drain output character and volume (feculent, bilious, cloudy, or fecal-smelling), check vital signs for distributive shock pattern, and measure urine output. Calculate a MEWS (Modified Early Warning Score); a score ≥5 or rising trend over 4 hours mandates urgent CT imaging. IMAGE: Gold-standard: CT abdomen and pelvis with IV and oral (or rectal) water-soluble contrast. Key findings: extraluminal contrast extravasation (diagnostic), perianastomotic fluid collections with air bubbles, free intraperitoneal air or fluid, and bowel wall thickening. If CT unavailable or contraindicated, a water-soluble contrast enema (for colorectal anastomoses) or drain fluid amylase/bilirubin assay (for upper GI) can provide supportive evidence. Bedside ultrasound may demonstrate localized perianastomotic collections but has low sensitivity for microleaks. DECIDE: Grade A (contained, no sepsis): conservative management--NPO, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g 8-hourly), CT-guided percutaneous drainage if collection >3 cm, and parenteral nutrition. Grade B (contained with sepsis): as above plus endoscopic or endoluminal vacuum therapy (E-VAC) if accessible. Grade C (free leak with peritonitis/sepsis): emergency re-laparotomy, takedown of anastomosis, end stoma, peritoneal lavage, and ICU admission. In Kenyan settings where interventional radiology and endoscopic vacuum therapy may not be available, a lower threshold for re-laparotomy is justified--'if you think it might be leaking, it probably is; if you're sure, it's already advanced.'

managementPearls.pearl: Tachycardia out of proportion to volume status is the SINGLE most reliable early sign of anastomotic leak. Do NOT be falsely reassured by a normal abdominal exam, normothermia, or white cell count in the first 48 hours.
A falling CRP from day 2 to day 3 followed by a secondary rise on day 4-5 is highly suggestive of leak (positive likelihood ratio ~6.5). Track CRP as a trend, not a snapshot.

In Kenyan settings where CT is not immediately available, the 'tachycardia + rising CRP + failure of ileus to resolve' triad has a positive predictive value >85% for significant leak requiring reintervention.

When performing re-laparotomy for leak, resist the temptation to re-suture or re-staple the leaking anastomosis. The tissue is edematous, ischemic, and infected--take it down and construct an end stoma. Attempted repair in this setting carries a >50% risk of re-leak.

Intra-operative assessment of anastomotic integrity: fill the pelvis with saline and insufflate air via a proctoscope (for colorectal anastomoses) or inject methylene blue via a nasogastric tube (for upper GI) to test the 'dunk test.'

Pre-operative optimization: correct hypoalbuminemia (target >30 g/L) with parenteral nutrition for 5-7 days before elective colorectal surgery in malnourished patients. In the Kenyan setting, consider a 7-day course of high-protein oral nutritional supplements (Ensure, Fortisip) before elective surgery.

Peri-operative oxygen therapy: maintain FiO2 0.8 intra-operatively and for 6 hours postoperatively to reduce anastomotic leak (PROXI trial data). Avoid vasopressors if possible--norepinephrine reduces splanchnic perfusion by 20-30% at standard doses.

When a contained anastomotic leak is detected (Grade A), attempt conservative management first: NPO, nasogastric decompression, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Meropenem), CT-guided percutaneous drainage of any fluid collection >3 cm, and parenteral nutrition. Success rate: 60-80% for contained leaks without sepsis. Check drain amylase daily -- a falling trend is reassuring.

For a Grade B leak (contained with sepsis), consider endoscopic options if available: endoluminal vacuum therapy (E-VAC) for rectal leaks, or fully covered self-expanding metal stents (SEMS) for esophageal and gastric leaks. E-VAC achieves closure in 80-90% of rectal leaks within 3-6 weeks. SEMS for esophageal leaks has lower success (60-70%) but can be a bridge to surgery.

In Kenyan district hospitals where CT, interventional radiology, and endoscopic therapy are unavailable, base the decision to cooperate on the 'clinical triad': tachycardia >100 + rising CRP + failure of ileus to resolve by POD4. If all three are present, proceed to laparotomy without imaging. Delaying surgery to arrange transfer to a tertiary center increases mortality.

operativeNuances.nuance: Anastomotic technique--choose the correct layer: For small bowel, a single layer of interrupted 3-0 Vicryl (or PDS) seromuscular bites 4-5 mm apart, 5-8 mm from the cut edge, with knots on the serosal surface, provides equivalent strength to a two-layer closure but with less luminal narrowing. For colon, a single layer of interrupted 3-0 PDS (longer absorption time) is preferred in contaminated fields. For the esophagus, use 3-0 Maxon or PDS interrupted sutures in a single layer--silk and Vicryl degrade too quickly in the acidic mediastinal environment. In resource-limited Kenyan operating theaters, where newer monofilament sutures may be unavailable, 2-0 silk in two layers (connector layer full-thickness + seromuscular Lembert) is an acceptable alternative--though with a 1.5× higher leak rate.

Bowel end viability assessment: The single most reliable indicator is arterial bleeding from the cut edge (not venous ooze). Use pulsatile bright red blood from the submucosal vessels as the benchmark. Check this BEFORE fashioning the anastomosis. If the edge does not bleed briskly, resect further until a viable margin is obtained. Perfusion assessment using indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence angiography is ideal but rarely available in Kenya; clinical judgment with observation of color (pink not dusky), temperature (warm not cold), and peristalsis is a reasonable surrogate.

Tension-free anastomosis is non-negotiable: If the two bowel ends come together under ANY tension, mobilize further. For the left colon, fully mobilize the splenic flexure down to the pelvis. For the right colon, mobilize the hepatic flexure and duodenum. If still under tension after maximal mobilization, resect more colon rather than pulling--the blood supply is already compromised at the point of maximal tension. The 'rule of thumb' (literally): if you can oppose the ends comfortably with your thumb and index finger, tension is acceptable; if not, mobilize further.

Hand-sewn technique for challenging fields: In the presence of inflammation, edema, or frozen pelvis, use the 'difficult anastomosis' approach--start with a posterior row of seromuscular 3-0 PDS stay sutures placed 1 cm apart, then complete the full-thickness layer with Gambee sutures (incorporating all layers including serosa) tied on the mucosal side. This technique minimizes mucosal inversion and provides the most precise tissue apposition in edematous tissue.

Omental patch reinforcement: In high-risk anastomoses (low rectal, after neoadjuvant radiotherapy, emergency colonic), consider wrapping a viable omental pedicle flap (based on the left gastroepiploic arcade) around the anastomotic line. The omentum provides neovascularization, delivers immune cells, and mechanically seals microdefects. A Cochrane review showed a trend toward reduced leak rates (OR 0.67, 95% CI 0.42-1.06), though not statistically significant.

At re-laparotomy for an anastomotic leak, the most critical decision is whether to take down the anastomosis or leave it. Decision algorithm:
(1) Generalized peritonitis + septic shock → take down, end stoma
(2) Localized peritonitis + contained collection → take down if >50% circumference is dehisced; repair with proximal diverting stoma if <30% circumference defect in well-vascularized tissue
(3) No peritonitis + contained leak → leave anastomosis, drain collection, proximal diversion
(4) Necrotic bowel at anastomosis → resect, end stoma, do NOT re-anastomose

Technical tips for safe loop stoma creation after anastomotic leak: bring the proximal limb out through a separate trephine incision (not the midline wound) to minimize surgical site infection and hernia. Mature the stoma primarily at the skin level (Brooke eversion). Ensure the stoma passes through the rectus sheath to reduce parastomal hernia. The distal (defunctionalized) limb can be closed at the fascial level or brought out as a mucous fistula if long enough.

prognosis.stomaNonReversal_Rate: 15-25% (higher in low-resource settings due to loss to follow-up)
prognosis.longTerm_QualityOfLife: Patients surviving anastomotic leak have significantly worse long-term QoL scores (EORTC QLQ-CR29), particularly in body image, defecation frequency, and social function domains. Stoma non-reversal contributes substantially to this decrement.
prognosis.recurrenceRisk: Anastomotic stricture at the take-down site occurs in 10-15% of leak survivors after stoma reversal, requiring endoscopic dilation in most cases.
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Time from onset to intervention is the single most modifiable determinant of mortality (every 6-hour delay beyond 12 hours increases mortality by ~8%). Surgeon experience (<50 colorectal anastomoses independently performed) doubles the leak rate. Available resources (CT access, ICU beds, interventional radiology capacity) significantly influence the ability to offer grade-appropriate management.
